figures.caption: Figure 12: Split-free ablation at ρ = 1.5. Rows report target regret and robust regret, while columns correspond to Scenarios 1 and 2. PROWL (split-free) fits nuisance functions and the policy on the same sample, whereas PROWL (sample-split) uses a stratified honest half-sample split.

Figure 9: Complementary performance comparison across varying sample sizes (N) for Scenario 1. The left panel reports target regret against the R-family baselines, and the right panel reports robust regret against the R-family baselines.

Figure 3: Performance comparison across varying sample sizes (N) for Scenario 1. The left panel reports target regret against the R-family baselines, and the right panel reports robust regret against the R-family baselines.

Figure 14: Procedural sensitivity to the preference-uncertainty radius ρ in the actual-data experiment. The panels report the estimated certified value, composite-free value, and alert rate for PROWL, PROWL (U = 0), Q-learning (R), and Q-learning (R) across varying levels of ρ. While a larger ρ structurally induces a more conservative lower utility, PROWL strategically responds by modestly reducing its alerting frequency, consistently preserving the clinically anchored composite-free value.

Figure 13: Hospital-level intention-to-treat heterogeneity in the ELAIA-1 trial. The plotted quantity is the risk difference of alert versus control on the 14-day composite outcome, accompanied by Wald-type 95% confidence intervals. Positive values favor usual care. The two non-teaching hospitals exhibit the clearest adverse alert signal, strongly motivating the use of site-aware individualized alerting rules.

Figure 10: Complementary performance comparison across varying sample sizes (N) for Scenario 2. The left panel reports target regret against the R-family baselines, and the right panel reports robust regret against the R-family baselines.

Figure 2: Performance comparison across varying uncertainty levels (ρ) for Scenario 2. The left panel reports target regret against the R-family baselines, and the right panel reports robust regret against the R-family baselines.

Table 1: Repeated sample-split AIPW comparison on the ELAIA-1 AKI-alert trial under the three-component uncertain hard-clinical utility with ρ = 1. Entries are means with standard errors over 30 repeated 70/30 hospital-by-alert stratified splits. Certified value is the estimate of VR, nominal value is the corresponding estimate of VR, and composite-free value uses Rcomp = 1 − composite outcome. Among adaptive learned policies, PROWL attains the largest certified value and the largest composite-free value.

Figure 1: Performance comparison across varying uncertainty levels (ρ) for Scenario 1. The left panel reports target regret against the R-family baselines, and the right panel reports robust regret against the R-family baselines.

Figure 11: Certificate-diagnostic plots. Rows report the proxy-target gap V (d̂) − V ∗(d̂) and the target-certified gap V ∗(d̂)− VR(d̂), while columns correspond to Scenarios 1 and 2. Methods shown are PROWL, PROWL (U = 0), and the classical R-family baselines.

Figure 5: Point-range summary of the primary actual-data comparison on ELAIA-1. Left panel reports the estimated certified value V̂R with 95% confidence intervals across the 30 repeated sample splits. Right panel reports the estimated composite-free value V̂comp with the same uncertainty summary. The figure complements Table 1 by emphasizing method ordering on the certified objective and on the trial’s primary hard-outcome anchor.

Figure 7: Complementary performance comparison across varying uncertainty levels (ρ) for Scenario 1. The left panel reports target regret against the R-family baselines, and the right panel reports robust regret against the R-family baselines.

Table 3: Component-wise decomposition of the repeated sample-split AIPW evaluation on ELAIA-1. Entries are means with standard errors over 30 repeated 70/30 hospital-by-alert stratified splits. This table complements Table 1 by demonstrating that the improvements in certified value are not driven by deterioration in any single hard-outcome component.

Table 2: Dataset-level certificate diagnostics across uncertainty levels ρ. Here E[U ] is the logged-sample mean certificate magnitude, Clip is the logged-sample rate P(U > R), and Valid is the independent-test validity rate that the oracle envelope upper-bounds the true proxy bias in both arms.

Figure 15: Ablation sensitivity analysis excluding hospital covariates. For each method, the points and intervals compare the baseline analysis against a refitted model that completely removes hospital indicators from the policy covariates. Although absolute performance experiences a slight drop across all methods, the relative ordering remains robustly unchanged. This confirms that the observed gains of PROWL are not driven merely by the memorization of site-specific labels.

Figure 8: Complementary performance comparison across varying uncertainty levels (ρ) for Scenario 2. The left panel reports target regret against the R-family baselines, and the right panel reports robust regret against the R-family baselines.

Figure 6: Learned alert allocation by baseline-risk quintile and hospital type. Baseline-risk quintiles are obtained within each training fold by fitting a control-arm risk model for the primary composite outcome and then evaluating the resulting risk scores on the held-out fold. Each panel displays the mean recommended alert rate across the repeated sample splits for one policy. Teaching hospitals are shown in blue and non-teaching hospitals in red. PROWL is compared against the strongest raw-reward baseline, Q-learning (R), and the strongest lower-reward plug-in baseline, Q-learning (R).

Figure 4: Performance comparison across varying sample sizes (N) for Scenario 1. The left panel reports target regret against the R-family baselines, and the right panel reports robust regret against the R-family baselines.
